unit.name: LSTM history, memory state, and gates
unit.description: Introduces LSTMs as a memory-preserving RNN with a cell state, a hidden state, and gating vectors.
unit.summary: This segment introduces Long Short-Term Memory networks as the response to the short-memory problem of simple RNNs, including the historical note that the core idea dates to Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997) and that the forget gate was added in later work that shaped the modern form [ts=1421s] [ts=1557s]. The architecture adds a cell state alongside the hidden state, then computes three sigmoid gate vectors—forget, input, and output—from the previous hidden state and the current input [ts=1739s] [ts=1774s]. The lecture walks through the update logic: a candidate cell update is computed, the old cell content is retained according to the forget gate, new content is written according to the input gate, and the hidden state is formed by applying an output gate to tanh of the new cell state [ts=1917s] [ts=1946s]. It also clarifies the interpretation of the cell as the primary memory and the hidden state as the part exposed for immediate generation/prediction [ts=2052s] [ts=2077s].

key_points.text: LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory and was introduced to extend the short memory of simple RNNs.
Modern LSTMs include a forget gate that was not present in the original 1997 version.
An LSTM maintains both a hidden state and a cell state.
The forget, input, and output gates are sigmoid vectors between 0 and 1 that control information flow.
The cell update combines remembered old cell content and gated candidate new content via elementwise products and addition.
The hidden state is produced by gating tanh of the cell state, exposing only some memory for current prediction.

unit_summary_for_generation: This segment introduces Long Short-Term Memory networks as the response to the short-memory problem of simple RNNs, including the historical note that the core idea dates to Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997) and that the forget gate was added in later work that shaped the modern form [ts=1421s] [ts=1557s]. The architecture adds a cell state alongside the hidden state, then computes three sigmoid gate vectors—forget, input, and output—from the previous hidden state and the current input [ts=1739s] [ts=1774s]. The lecture walks through the update logic: a candidate cell update is computed, the old cell content is retained according to the forget gate, new content is written according to the input gate, and the hidden state is formed by applying an output gate to tanh of the new cell state [ts=1917s] [ts=1946s]. It also clarifies the interpretation of the cell as the primary memory and the hidden state as the part exposed for immediate generation/prediction [ts=2052s] [ts=2077s].

kp_catalog.name: LSTM gates and cell state
kp_catalog.description: Gated recurrent architecture that selectively writes, forgets, outputs, and preserves information.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because lSTM gates and cell state formalizes how gated recurrent architecture that selectively writes, forgets, outputs, and preserves information, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
